ideas.Suspension of judgment (epoché):
**Suspension of judgment** (*epoché*) is the central method and defining stance of the **skepticism** founded by Pyrrho of Elis — the practice of *withholding assent*, of neither affirming nor denying any claim about the true nature of things, in recognition that we cannot attain certain knowledge of how things really are. It is the pivotal move in the Pyrrhonist path to tranquility, and one of the most influential ideas in the history of epistemology.

Pyrrho and his followers observed that for virtually any question about the real nature of things, **equally strong arguments and appearances** can be marshaled on opposing sides — a state they called *isostheneia*, the "equal force" of contradictory positions. The same honey seems sweet to the healthy and bitter to the sick; the same tower looks round from afar and square from near; philosophers of every school offer conflicting dogmas, each with plausible support. Confronted with this endless, unresolvable conflict of appearances and arguments, the skeptic concludes that we have no rational basis for deciding which side is *true* — reality "in itself" remains inaccessible.

The proper response, Pyrrho taught, is **epoché**: to *suspend judgment*, refraining from committing to any belief or dogma about the real nature of things. The skeptic continues to note how things *appear* ("the honey appears sweet to me"), but withholds assent to any claim about how they *really are* ("but whether it is *really* sweet, I suspend judgment"). Crucially, this suspension is not a doctrine to be dogmatically asserted (which would be self-refuting) but a practical stance reached through experience. And it has a profound practical payoff: by giving up the anxious, frustrated pursuit of certainty and the dogmatic attachment to beliefs, the skeptic attains **tranquility** (*ataraxia*) — the ultimate goal of the Pyrrhonist life.
ideas.Things are equally indifferent, unmeasur:
A core teaching attributed to Pyrrho of Elis holds that **things themselves are, by nature, indeterminate** — "equally indifferent, unmeasurable, and undecidable" — and that this is precisely why we must suspend judgment about them and can thereby attain tranquility. Preserved in an important report by Pyrrho's follower Timon (via later authors), this threefold characterization expresses the metaphysical or epistemological foundation of Pyrrhonist skepticism.

The report advises that anyone who wishes to be happy must consider three questions: what things are like by nature; how we should be disposed toward them; and what will result from this. Pyrrho's answer to the first is that things are "**equally indifferent** (*adiaphora*), **unmeasurable** (*astathmēta*), **and undecidable** (*anepikrita*)." That is, things in their real nature do not differ determinately (they are not really, in themselves, one way rather than another); they cannot be measured or stabilized by our faculties; and no judgment about them can be conclusively decided or settled. Whether this is a claim about the indeterminate nature of *reality itself*, or (more cautiously) a claim about our *inability to determine* how things really are, is a matter of longstanding scholarly debate.

From this follows the answer to the second question — how we should be **disposed**: precisely because things are undifferentiated and undecidable, our sensations and opinions "neither tell the truth nor lie," so we should place no trust in them but be **without opinion, without inclination, and unwavering** (*adoxastoi, aklineis, akradantoi*), saying of each thing that it "no more is than is not." And the answer to the third question — the **result** — is that from this suspension of judgment there arises first "speechlessness" and then **tranquility** (*ataraxia*). This compact statement thus lays out the whole Pyrrhonist path: from the indeterminacy of things, through suspension of judgment, to inner peace.
ideas.Tranquility through giving up dogma:
The ultimate aim of Pyrrho of Elis's philosophy was not knowledge but **tranquility** (*ataraxia*) — a profound, unshakable peace of mind — and his startling discovery was that this tranquility is attained precisely by **giving up the quest for knowledge and the attachment to dogma**. This linkage of skepticism to inner peace is Pyrrhonism's most distinctive and influential insight, and it makes skepticism not merely a theoretical stance but a *therapeutic way of life*.

The reasoning runs as follows. Human beings, Pyrrho observed, are made anxious and miserable by their **dogmatic beliefs** — their firm convictions about what is truly good and bad, and about how things really are. We torment ourselves striving to attain what we believe is genuinely good and to avoid what we believe is genuinely bad; we are disturbed and frustrated by the endless, unresolvable disputes of the philosophers and by our inability to reach certainty. The dogmatist, committed to definite beliefs, is perpetually agitated — troubled when things go against his convictions, and anxious in the very holding of them.

The skeptic's path to peace is to **suspend judgment** (*epoché*) — to stop asserting that things are really good or bad, really one way or another — and thereby to release the anxiety that dogmatic commitment breeds. Once one recognizes that no belief can be conclusively established, and gives up the futile insistence on certainty, the disturbance ceases. There is a famous story that Pyrrho's followers discovered this connection almost by accident: seeking to resolve their disputes and finding they could not, they gave up and suspended judgment — and found that tranquility "followed as if by chance, as a shadow follows a body." Giving up dogma, they found the peace that seeking knowledge had denied them. This vision of skepticism as a cure for anxiety profoundly shaped later thought.

works.Wrote nothing; doctrines preserved by Se:
Like Socrates, whom he resembled in this respect, **Pyrrho of Elis wrote nothing** — he left behind no treatises or writings of any kind, embodying his philosophy in the way he *lived* rather than in any text. This is fitting for a thinker whose entire outlook counseled the **suspension of judgment** and the abandonment of dogmatic assertion: a man who withheld assent to all doctrines was perhaps unlikely to commit doctrines to writing. Pyrrho's philosophy was transmitted instead through his personal example, his disciples, and the reports of later authors.

Our knowledge of Pyrrho therefore comes entirely **indirectly**. The most important early source was his devoted pupil **Timon of Phlius**, who wrote (in satirical verses called the *Silloi* and other works) preserving and celebrating his master's teachings and character; Timon's works, too, survive only in fragments and quotations. Later, the biographer **Diogenes Laërtius** devoted a chapter to Pyrrho, preserving many anecdotes about his life and his imperturbable demeanor — the stories of his serene indifference to danger and discomfort that made him a legend of tranquility.

Above all, the systematic philosophical content of **Pyrrhonism** was preserved and elaborated centuries later by **Sextus Empiricus** (2nd–3rd century CE), whose surviving works — especially the *Outlines of Pyrrhonism* — are our fullest and most important source for the skeptical tradition that bore Pyrrho's name. It is important to note, however, that the developed, systematic Pyrrhonism of Sextus (with its elaborate "modes" and methods) was the product of a long tradition of later skeptics, and may differ considerably from the historical Pyrrho's own, simpler outlook. Reconstructing what Pyrrho himself actually thought — as opposed to what the later school made of him — is thus a delicate task, since everything we have passes through the filter of these later transmitters.
